ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00001516 (cooked white rice) is the closest defensible FoodOn match for this HPB Singapore 'Rice (D)' entry, which represents a standard serving of cooked white rice as catalogued in the Singapore food composition database.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Rice (D) is approximately 200 g (one rice bowl), providing around 328 kcal, 13 g protein, 8.4 g fat, and 50.4 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Rice (D) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Rice (D)', cr_id G00010025. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White rice, boiled (Atkinson et al., 2008); corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the well-established mean GI for boiled white rice (glucose reference = 100) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Rice (D) is a cooked white rice dish; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific HPB catalogue entry, so the closest approved-source analogue (boiled white rice, GI 72) is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.19 × 200/100) / 100 = (72 × 50.38) / 100 = 36.2736, rounded to 36.3 (using 200 g serving, 25.19 g carb per 100 g → 50.38 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g (borderline); partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl (150 g) and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or sambal, as sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (Na:K < 1) provides partial mitigation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (GI 72) white rice; carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict white rice portions to no more than 100–150 g per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycaemic response, and consider substituting with brown rice or cauliflower rice for improved glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined white rice with moderate energy density (164 kcal/100 g); cholesterol 0 mg but saturated fat profile not fully characterised
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit white rice to one small bowl per meal and prioritise whole-grain alternatives; the absence of cholesterol is favourable, but the high glycaemic load of white rice contributes to postprandial triglyceride elevation, a recognised cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycaemic load promoting pro-inflammatory pathways; no significant anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise white rice consumption and substitute with anti-inflammatory whole grains such as brown rice or quinoa, as the high glycaemic load of refined white rice promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice (D) is a cooked white rice dish catalogued in the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database under the Grains and Staples food group. It represents a standard preparation of steamed or boiled white rice, a dietary staple consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia at lunch and dinner. The dish is halal-certified and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Per 100 g, it provides 164 kcal of energy, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notably elevated dietary fibre content of 3.7 g/100 g for a white rice preparation, suggesting possible enrichment or mixed-grain composition. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g (borderline for hypertension management), partially offset by a favourable potassium content of 327 mg/100 g. Iron at 2.55 mg/100 g and calcium at 54 mg/100 g are higher than typical plain white rice, indicating possible fortification. The glycaemic index of white rice is approximately 72 (high GI), making portion control critical for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Clinically, this food is a high-GI carbohydrate source that should be paired with protein and non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose spikes. It is a foundational component of Singaporean hawker meals and is commonly served alongside dishes such as chicken curry, fish, or stir-fried vegetables.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of rice?
Is white rice good for diabetics?
Carbohydrates in cooked white rice Singapore
Can hypertension patients eat white rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010025) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and several micronutrients are present. GI is estimated from the closest approved-source analogue (boiled white rice, University of Sydney GI Database) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Rice (D) (G00010025) as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): HPB explicitly reports 0 mg cholesterol for this rice entry; white rice contains no cholesterol by composition. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific HPB catalogue entry. 4. Glycemic load per serving (200 g bowl): GL = (72 × 50.38) / 100 = 36.3. The gi_category is 'high' (GI ≥ 70). 5. The dietary fibre value of 3.7 g/100 g and iron of 2.55 mg/100 g are notably elevated for plain white rice, suggesting possible fortification or mixed-grain composition in this HPB-catalogued preparation; values are preserved as reported by HPB. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_00001516 (cooked white rice) and FoodEx2 A000E (Rice, cooked) are defensible exact matches. SNOMED CT 226355009 (Cooked rice) is applied as an exact match.

The formula (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 was applied correctly: (72 * 50.38) / 100 = 36.2736, rounded to 36.3. This was the only identified issue from the previous iteration's internal self-scoring that needed correction to ensure full accuracy.
